column8: AI can be trained and nearly always is leans to changing behaviour to better appeal to the person using it and therefore it can lose its use as a helpful interactive mental health journal. There is also the fact that there has been past cases of ai negatively impacting people, usually minors or even older teens,  to the point of suicide in the most extreme cases or becoming more socially isolated in more common cases.

column14: A concern would be that struggling people may actually view the ai tool as “the complete solution” not a “a partial solution” … what this means is they read a comforting response, but completely miss a beneficial solution the ai gives somewhere in between the comforting. For example the ai is capable of giving mental therapy but it can only “suggest” behavioural therapy, so the person may think they’ve done enough therapy but they’ve completely ignored the behavioural solution. the comforting ai is an incomplete partial solution for mental health problems that require more behavioural therapy than mental therapy. Overall the ai could prevent people from seeking exposure therapy which is the therapy that gives a struggling person the practice and experience needed to prevent avoidance (the ai gives) and produce productivity/workforce participation.

column7: It might be helpful in providing a wider range of potential help to individuals that may not be able to easily access 1-on-1 therapy. Like if a person is dealing with a particular issue and they feel they can't go to a friend, it may be useful for them to be able to unload their thoughts on an ai diary.
column8: I think the main ethical issue with an ai system like this is confidentiality. If its core basis is to respond to what's being written, how is that data then going to be stored, would it compare other people's responses to provide a suggestion? Another is protection from harm. Would the Ai be able to completely detect if an individual's mental health is at a critical point and intervene where necessary like a human would?
column9: I think there are quite a number of people this could help, for example those who feel like their emotions are a burden to people close to them and so struggle to express the depth/severity of their emotions and thoughts. Or if someone just feels like they need a simple outlet, they could use the Ai as another "person" to talk to.

column11: Extremely important — I wouldn't use it without strong privacy guarantees
column14: A slight concern is if the ai would be able to completely adapt to create new responses that are entirely relevant to how the person feels, or would it eventually run out of responses and reuse them if it's limited to how much it could access and generate. Another slight concern is that even if people are aware that this isn't a complete alternative to professional care, what methods are being put in place to prevent a complete dependency/reliance being built on the Ai?

column7: AI could be useful for consolidating input given by a patient. However given it’s tendency to steer it’s responses towards enabling the user, it’s more likely to encourage destructive self management actions than to help halt them. So resigning AI to a consolidation tool would be it’s most monitor-able function and most helpful, given a sturdy predictability.
column8: Safeguarding is a huge concern. As stated in the text box above, given AIs tendency to enable struggling individuals, when applied in a psychological sense it could great a destructive echo-chamber for patients as opposed to helping them.
column9: If AI were to be applied in any form of the psychological help process, the most beneficial form would be in triaging patients and consolidating information to help direct them to the most helpful team for the patient
